coredata.dc:title: Photothermal effects of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels under near-infrared stimulation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Gao, Jing
Zhang, Qi
Wang, Xiaoyuan
Li, Huaqian
Guo, Xing
Han, Dan
Wang, Xiaojuan
Xi, Yanfeng
Guo, Li
Li, Pengcui
Xue, Juanjuan
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: In this study, supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogel dressings for the photothermal treatment of melanoma were successfully developed. The prepared supra-CNDs (supra-carbon nanodots) exhibited remarkable photothermal properties, with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 55.29 % (47.12 %) under 730 nm (808 nm) laser irradiation, which is a promising material for photothermal reagents. The integrated GelMA (gelatine methacryloyl hydrogel) further enhanced the photothermal effects of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels and also shortened the exposure time, which could rapidly reach 50 ℃ within 70 s under 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2). Additionally, the thermal conductivity of the composite hydrogels was investigated at a laser spot diameter of 2 mm. Moreover, the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels exhibited good biocompatibility and excellent photothermal ablation of melanoma cells. These findings demonstrate the potential of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels as dressings for the photothermal treatment of skin cancers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melanoma
Photothermal therapy
Supra-CNDs
GelMA
Hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of supra-CNDs and GelMA hydrogels
Preparation of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels
Photothermal ablation of composite hydrogels on cancer cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, U21A20353 ), Key Research and Development Program of Shanxi Province ( 202202040201012, 20220210101004 ), the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province ( 202103021224081, 202203021212261 ) and the Key medical scientific research projects in Shanxi Province (NO. 2021XM12 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of supra-CNDs. Inset: particle size distribution of supra-CNDs. (b) HRTEM of supra-CNDs. SEM image of GelMA hydrogels (G12) (c) and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels (d). Inset: pore size distribution of G12 and composite hydrogels. The swelling rates (e) and stress-strain curves (f) of the supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels with different concentrations of supra-CNDs (0, 200, 500, 700, and 1000 μg/mL) doped into the G12 solution.
Absorption spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b), and emission spectra at 360 nm excitation (c) of supra-CNDs, GelMA and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels.

Photothermal effects of irradiating different concentrations of supra-CNDs solutions with 730 nm (a) and 808 nm (c) lasers (2 W/cm2). Photothermal effects of irradiating supra-CNDs solutions with different irradiation powers of 730 nm (b) and 808 nm (d) lasers.

Photothermal effects of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels in different states: aqueous solutions irradiated by 730 nm (a) and 808 nm (d) lasers (2 W/cm2) and gel state irradiated by 730 nm (b) and 808 nm (e) lasers (2 W/cm2). Infrared thermogram of the composite hydrogels under the gel after 100 s of 730 nm (c) and 808 nm (f) laser irradiation.

(a) Photothermal effect at different distances from the centre of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels when irradiated vertically with an 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2). (b) Schematic of the laser irradiation position. (c) Temperature changes of composite hydrogels with a radius of 2 mm when irradiated under an 808 nm laser with different irradiation powers.

Relative survival of A-375 cells cocultured with different concentrations of GelMA hydrogels (a) and supra-CNDs (b). Relative survival of A-375 cells (c), A-375 cells cocultured with GelMA (G12) (d) and supra-CNDs (700 μg/mL) (e) irradiated with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers (2 W/cm2) for 5 min. (f) Relative survival of A-375 cells irradiated with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers (2 W/cm2) cocultured with supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels for 1 min.

GelMA (12 %) hydrogels doped with different concentrations of supra-CNDs were cocultured with A-375 cells for 24 h. After irradiation with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 1 min, fluorescence imaging was performed with calcein-AM/PI dye. Surviving cells are coloured green, and dead cells are coloured red. The scale bar is300 μm.
Schematic diagram of the formation and application of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels.

head.ce:title: Photothermal effects of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels under near-infrared stimulation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jing Gao and Qianqian Duan contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogel dressings for the photothermal treatment of melanoma were successfully developed. The prepared supra-CNDs (supra-carbon nanodots) exhibited remarkable photothermal properties, with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 55.29 % (47.12 %) under 730 nm (808 nm) laser irradiation, which is a promising material for photothermal reagents. The integrated GelMA (gelatine methacryloyl hydrogel) further enhanced the photothermal effects of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels and also shortened the exposure time, which could rapidly reach 50 ℃ within 70 s under 808 nm laser irradiation (1 W/cm2). Additionally, the thermal conductivity of the composite hydrogels was investigated at a laser spot diameter of 2 mm. Moreover, the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels exhibited good biocompatibility and excellent photothermal ablation of melanoma cells. These findings demonstrate the potential of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels as dressings for the photothermal treatment of skin cancers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The skin, which is the largest organ of the human body, serves as the primary defence against foreign invaders. However, it is also the most vulnerable organ, and melanoma is one of the most prevalent and severe forms of skin cancer [1–5]. Currently, the main treatments for melanoma include chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery. Because melanoma is highly resistant to treatments such as chemotherapy, researchers have explored photodynamic therapy and photothermal therapy (PTT) to eradicate tumour cells [6–9]. Photothermal therapy provides an effective method for selectively eliminating cancerous tissues without harming the surrounding healthy cells. PTT is a noninvasive treatment for tumors that uses a near-infrared (NIR) laser to irradiate the tumor site, converting light energy into heat energy to cause irreversible cellular damage. PTT has become a very beneficial method in tumor treatment due to its short treatment time, less toxic side effects and obvious therapeutic effects [10–12].

Currently, the main photothermal conversion materials include noble metal-based nanomaterials, organic NIR chromophores, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes [13–16]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) stand out due to their unique optical properties, high water dispersion, good biocompatibility, easy surface modification, low cost, and environmental friendliness [17]. Photothermal agent supra-CNDs have been developed based on conventional CNDs, which have strong absorption in the visible-NIR region and a high photothermal conversion efficiency of up to 50 % [18–20].

As melanoma is a skin tumor, the treatment process inevitably causes damage to the skin surface. The treatment process therefore needs to be able to treat melanoma without toxic side effects and simultaneously promote the regeneration of the wound [21–25]. To ensure the effectiveness of photothermal therapy while enhancing the elimination of deep tumor cells, researchers have discovered that photothermal reagents can be physically loaded or chemically grafted onto dressings, thus proposing a therapeutic strategy that combines photothermal reagents with dressings [26]. Furthermore, it was found that the photothermal effect of the photothermal agent could be enhanced by mixing the appropriate concentration of photothermal reagent in the hydrogel [27–30]. Among them, hydrogels [31] have emerged as one of the most promising options due to their excellent three-dimensional structure and ability to maintain a moist environment, in contrast to micelles [32], composite membranes [33] and others [34]. Gelatine is derived from the hydrolysis of natural collagen, which exhibits outstanding biocompatibility and is widely used in the preparation of hydrogels [35–37]. Therefore, gelatine has significant potential for the development of hydrogels for combined photothermal therapy and wound dressings.

In this study, CNDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method with dicyandiamide and citric acid. After self-assembly for 24 h at 60 % humidity and 4 °C, supra-CNDs were formed. By optimizing the reaction temperature and time, the optimal absorption effect of supra-CNDs can be achieved in the near infrared absorption band. Supra-CNDs aqueous solutions were used as solvents to dissolve GelMA solid and then photocrosslinked to form supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels. The concentration ratio of supra-CNDs and GelMA was optimized, and their photothermal properties, thermal conductivity, and photothermal ablation of tumor cells were studied. The excellent photothermal properties of photothermal reagents and biological dressings make them promising in the field of biomedicine in the future..

Citric acid (CA) (≥99.5 %) and dicyandiamide (≥98 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Taiyuan, China). Gelatine and methacrylic anhydride were acquired from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Lithium phenyl-2,4,6-trimethyl benzoyl phosphinate (LAP) was provided by StemEasy (Jiangsu, China). CCK-8 (Cell-Counting-Kit-8) and live-dead assay kits were acquired from BestBio (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), bovine serum (FBS) and antibiotic-antimycotic solution were purchased from Tian-hang Biochemical Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

The absorption spectra were measured by a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-8000A, 190–1200 nm, Metash, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by a JEOL-2100 F field emission TEM (JEOL, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured by an FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The surface functional groups of the samples were tested using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optik, Germany). The mechanical properties of the hydrogels were tested by an electronic universal material testing machine (Instron, USA). In the photothermal experiments, an 808 nm CW laser (MDL-III-808 nm, CNI, China) and a 730 nm CW laser (MW-GX-730 nm, Laser, China) were used as the irradiated sources. The temperature changes of all samples were recorded with an infrared thermal camera (225 s, Fotric, China). Cell imaging results were carried out by fluorescence microscopy (Leica Microsystems, Leica DM 3000, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of supra-CNDs and GelMA hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of supra-CNDs refers to the work of Si et al. [20]. 1 g of dicyandiamide and 0.25 g of citric acid were mixed with 16 mL of DI water and then transferred into a 50 mL high temperature reactor and heated at 200 ℃ for 4 h. Freeze-drying yielded CNDs powder. After the freeze-dried yellow-green powder was placed in an environment with a humidity of 60 % and a temperature of 4 ℃ for 24 h, the dark green supra-CNDs powders could be obtained.

10 g of gelatine was dissolved in 100 mL of PBS solution, and the mixture was stirred in a 50 ℃ water bath. When it was completely dissolved, the water bath was transferred to a fume hood, and 0.5 mL of methacrylate was added 16 times per minute (8 mL total). After stirring, 400 mL of PBS solution was added and stirred for 10 min. The stirred solution was placed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 8000–14000 Da) and dialyzed at 50 °C for 7 days. The dialyzed supernatant was centrifuged and freeze-dried to obtain GelMA solid, which was stored at −20 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: GelMA solid (0.32, 0.4, 0.48 and 0.6 g) was dissolved in 4 mL of supra-CNDs solution (1 mg/mL). Then, 0.3 % blue photoinitiator (LAP) was added. After complete dissolution, supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels with concentrations of 8 %, 10 %, 12 % and 15 % (G8, G10, G12, G15) were obtained by pouring into the model and curing with a 405 nm UV lamp.

The supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels were freeze-dried prior to testing. The weight of the freeze-dried samples was recorded as W
0 . The freeze-dried samples were soaked in deionized water for a period of time, weighed at regular intervals and recorded as W
s . Finally, the swelling rate (S
r ) was calculated as follows (n = 3):
S
r
=
(
W
s
−
W
0
W
0
)
×
100 %

The mechanical properties of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels were measured with an Instron 3343 (Instron Instruments, USA). The thickness and diameter of the composite hydrogels were recorded. The samples were placed on the testing platform with a preload force of 0.001 N and compressed at a strain rate of 0.8 mm/min.

The supra-CNDs were diluted to different concentrations (0.2, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 mg/mL) and poured into quartz cuvettes. The photothermal properties of the supra-CNDs were measured after irradiation with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min. Different laser power densities (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5 W/cm2) were used to study the laser power dose effect. To observe the temperature changes, an infrared camera was used to record the temperature of the supra-CNDs in real time every 20 s

GelMA hydrogels with different concentrations (8 %, 10 %, 12 %, 15 %) and supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels made by blending different concentrations of supra-CNDs (0, 200, 500, 700, 1000 μg/mL) in a GelMA aqueous solution (12 %) were prepared. Cylindrical samples with a diameter of 7 mm and a thickness of 3 mm were irradiated with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers for 5 min and 100 s at a power density of 2 W/cm2. The photothermal properties of GelMA hydrogels as well as supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels were measured. To observe the temperature change, the real-time temperature of the hydrogels was recorded using an infrared camera every 30 and 10 s. Photothermal conversion efficiency calculated according to Sang et al. [38].

Composite hydrogel cylindrical samples with different sizes were prepared by doping supra-CNDs (1 mg/mL) with GelMA hydrogels (12 %). The centre of the sample was vertically irradiated with a laser of 808 nm. The temperature changes at different distances from the centre were recorded using infrared thermography.

The study evaluated the cytotoxicity of GelMA hydrogels and supra-CNDs solution by examining the activity of melanoma (A-375) cells coincubated with them. Coculture of GelMA hydrogels with cells for incubation according to the method described by Wu et al. [39]. Gel precursor concentrations (0 %, 8 %, 10 %, 12 % and 15 %) were dissolved in high-glucose cell medium containing 0.3 % LAP. A-375 cells were suspended in different concentrations of GelMA solution at equal cell density. Experiments were conducted in 24-well tissue culture plates using a UV lamp (405 nm) for photocuring crosslinking. 1 Ml of medium was added and left for the appropriate number of days (the medium was changed every two days). After incubation for 1, 4 and 7 days, the medium was changed to 300 μL CCK-8 reagent solution, and the cells were cultured in an incubator for 1 h. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm by adding 100 μL solution to a 96-well plate. Cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
cell viability =
[ (
A
s
−
A
b
) / (
A
c
−
A
b
) ]
×
100 %

In the above formula, A
s is the absorbance of the experimental well (including cells, medium, CCK-8 solution and drug solution), A
c is the absorbance of the control well (including cells, medium, CCK-8 solution and no drug), and A
b is the absorbance of the blank well (including medium, CCK-8 solution and no cell and drug).

A-375 cells at the same density were inoculated in 24-well plates and cultured with supra-CNDs solution at concentrations of 0, 200, 500, 700, and 1000 μg/mL for 24 h. CCK-8 reagent was also used to detect cell activity. By comparing the cell viability of A-375 cells after coincubation with different concentrations of GelMA hydrogels and supra-CNDs solutions, the effect of GelMA hydrogels and supra-CNDs solution on cytotoxicity was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal ablation of composite hydrogels on cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: A-375 cells were also used as experimental cells for photothermal experiments in vitro. Three groups of 24-well plates were inoculated with A-375 cells, each of which was exposed to a UV lamp (405 nm) after the addition of the GelMA hydrogels (12 %), supra-CNDs solution (700 μg/mL) and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels, respectively. After 24 h of coculture, the first two groups were irradiated with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers (2 W/cm2) for 5 min, and the composite hydrogel group was irradiated for 1 min. The cell survival rates were assessed using the CCK-8 reagent. Three groups of cells were stained with calcein-AM/PI dye. Subsequently, they were placed under an enzymatic marker for fluorescence imaging and analysis.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the supra-CNDs exhibited a well-dispersed and nearly spherical structure with an average particle size of 12 nm. The structure had clear uniform lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm, consistent with the planar lattice spacing of graphene (100) (Fig. 1b) [40]. The cross-sections of both GelMA hydrogels (Fig. 1c) and supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels (Fig. 1d) showed a loose and porous structure. The pore size of the composite hydrogels was significantly increased in comparison with that of the single GelMA hydrogels, indicating the successful synthesis of the composite hydrogels [41]. A larger pore size is beneficial for nutrient exchange and cell migration [42,43]. The water absorption capacity of hydrogels has a significant impact on nutrient transport. The doping of nanoparticles in the hydrogel decreased the swelling rate [44], and the experimental results confirmed that the swelling rate decreased with increasing concentrations of supra-CNDs (Fig. 1e). Mechanical properties are an important factor in maintaining the structure of hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 1f, the composite hydrogels could withstand more pressure than the hydrogels without supra-CNDs, indicating that the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels had better mechanical properties. This suggests that the incorporation of supra-CNDs improves the mechanical properties of GelMA. These findings also verified the successful construction of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels [45].

The reaction temperature and time effect the NIR absorption properties of supra-CNDs, thus the parameters were initially optimized (Fig. S1). The temperature of 200 ℃ and time of 4 h were determined to obtain a wide and strong NIR absorption band. The Fig. 2a shows the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the supra-CNDs, GelMA and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels. It was obvious that supra-CNDs exhibited a wide and strong absorption band centred at 662 nm due to the n-π* transition [18]. Instead of decreasing its absorption band, the combination of GelMA enhanced the absorption intensity, probably due to a colour-enhancing effect [46]. A blueshift was exhibited in the absorption spectrum due to the addition of GelMA polar groups [47].

The surface characteristics of the composite hydrogels were further characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). Comparing the FTIR spectra of supra-CNDs, GelMA and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels, the characteristic peaks at 1550–1800 cm−1 and 3308 cm−1 in supra-CNDs [18] and 1242 cm−1 and 3284 cm−1 in GelMA hydrogels [48] were reflected in the composite hydrogels, demonstrating the successful physical binding of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels.

The emission spectra of supra-CNDs, GelMA and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S3) showed that they all had excitation wavelength dependence, and their emission spectra changed with the excitation wavelength. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2c), the successful binding of fluorescent supra-CNDs enabled the visualization of GelMA hydrogels. The diminished fluorescence of the composite hydrogels may be due to the supra-CNDs being encapsulated by the hydrogels [49]. This also demonstrates the successful construction of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels.

The absorption spectra of the supra-CNDs and supra-CNDs@GelMA hydrogels effectively cover the NIR-Ⅰ region of the biological window, indicating their potential for NIR photothermal properties. From Fig. 3, when the concentration of supra-CNDs was 1 mg/mL, it could reach 52.9 °C (or 49.6 ℃) after 5 min of irradiation with a 730 nm (or 808 nm) (2 W/cm2) laser. According to the literature, a temperature above 40 ℃ is enough to kill cancer cells, and only 5 min can kill cancer cells when the cell temperature is maintained at approximately 50 ℃ [50]. The photothermal conversion efficiency of supra-CNDs solution was calculated to be 55.29 % (or 47.12 %) under 730 nm (or 808 nm) laser irradiation from the calculation formula (Fig. S4). The photothermal conversion efficiency is comparable to the literatures [19,20]. The above photothermal effects show that supra-CNDs can efficiently absorb light and convert it into adequate heat energy, so they have the potential to be used as photothermal reagents. Therefore, a suitable concentration of supra-CNDs (1 mg/mL) was selected for subsequent experimental studies. The photothermal effects of GelMA at different concentrations in both aqueous and gel states were observed under 730 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation, respectively (Fig. S5). There was no significant photothermal warming effect of GelMA at any concentration. Therefore, a more appropriate concentration of GelMA (12 %) was selected for subsequent experimental studies.

The photothermal effects of 12 % GelMA hydrogels doped with different concentrations of supra-CNDs in aqueous and gel states were investigated ( Fig. 4). By comparing the photothermal effects of supra-CNDs, GelMA and supra-CNDs@GelMA in different states (Fig. S6), it can be observed that when the concentration of doped supra-CNDs was 1 mg/mL, the aqueous solution irradiated with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers for 100 s reached temperatures of 30.1 ℃ and 28.7 ℃, respectively. The photothermal warming effect of the aqueous solution of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels was reduced compared to that of the supra-CNDs solution. It should be noted that in the gel state, the infrared thermograms (Fig. 4c and f) showed that the real-time temperatures after 100 s of 730 nm (or 808 nm) laser irradiation were 80.3 °C (or 68.2 ℃) when the concentration of doped supra-CNDs was 1 mg/mL. The above data show that the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels in the gel state significantly enhanced the photothermal warming effect compared to that of the supra-CNDs solution alone. According to the warming effect (Fig. S7) of supra-CNDs solution and supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels in different media (DI water, DMEM and cell medium), the photothermal effect was almost stability and the influence of the media is negligible. These results indicate that supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels can rapidly absorb light energy and convert it into thermal energy, demonstrating their potential as photothermal dressings.

Photothermal dressings require not only a photothermal warming effect but also photothermal conductivity, which is an important parameter for small-beam laser irradiation [51]. Therefore, at a laser spot diameter of approximately 2 mm, the photothermal capacity of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels was investigated ( Fig. 5). After 100 s of vertical irradiation of the centre of the composite hydrogels with an 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2), the centre temperature reached 73.2 °C. The temperature at a distance of 2 mm from the centre of the light spot was 60.5 °C, while the temperature at a 3.5 mm distance was only 49.8 °C. It was found that its temperature decreased gradually with increasing distance and even failed to meet the requirements of a good photothermal reagent. Therefore, to ensure both its excellent photothermal ability and size for the preparation study, supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels with a radius of 2 mm were prepared for subsequent study. Photothermal tests were conducted on samples with lasers at different power densities (0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 W/cm2), and it was found that the temperature could reach 54.4 °C after 100 s of irradiation at 1 W/cm2 power density (Fig. 5c).

Good biocompatibility and excellent photothermal ablation ability are prerequisites for use as PTT reagents. The biocompatibility of GelMA was evaluated by using melanoma cells (A-375) as models without laser irradiation ( Fig. 6a, Fig. S8). The cells cocultured with GelMA hydrogels showed good cell viability (above 90 %) according to the CCK-8 assay results, even at high GelMA concentrations (12 %) and long incubation times (7 days). Different concentrations of GelMA also showed high cellular viability rates (Fig. S9a). These findings suggested that GelMA hydrogels are virtually nonbiotoxic and well suited for use as wound dressings. The cell survival rates after laser irradiation of the GelMA hydrogels (G12) surface were all above 80 % (Fig. 6d), which showed that GelMA has almost no photothermal ablation capacity. In the absence of laser irradiation, supra-CNDs had good biocompatibility (Fig. 6b). The results showed that the cell survival rate after incubation with different concentrations of supra-CNDs aqueous solution was above 95 %, indicating that the supra-CNDs solution also has excellent biocompatibility. A more suitable concentration of supra-CNDs (700 μg/mL) was selected for the photothermal ablation experiments (Fig. 6e). 48 % (52 %) of the cells survived under 730 nm (or 808 nm) irradiation for 100 s. This result indicated that supra-CNDs possess certain photothermal ablation abilities.

The photothermal ablation capacity of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels was tested (Fig. 6f). The experimental results demonstrated a significant decrease in cell survival with increasing concentrations of doped supra-CNDs after laser irradiation. When the concentration of doped supra-CNDs was 700 μg/mL, the cell survival rates were 32 % and 29 % after 60 s of 730 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation, respectively. It is obvious that the photothermal ablation effect of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels is significantly enhanced compared with that of supra-CNDs and that the irradiation time is drastically shortened. Cell proliferation tests (Fig. S9b) also observed a gradual decrease in the number of cells with increasing doping concentration, which also demonstrated the excellent photothermal ablation ability of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels. In addition, the cell survival rates of the blank control group after irradiation with 730 nm and 808 nm lasers were 90 % and 93 %, respectively (Fig. 6c), proving that laser irradiation was almost harmless to the cells.

The photothermal ablation effect was visually displayed by using calcein-AM/PI staining ( Fig. 7). Under laser irradiation, the apoptosis rate of cells increased with increasing concentration. The visual analysis of photothermal ablation reaffirmed that supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels could be utilized as photothermal dressings and showed significant anticancer effects in vitro.

In summary, a photothermal therapy dressing system was successfully constructed by using supra-CNDs and GelMA hydrogels. The supra-CNDs solution exhibited a photothermal conversion efficiency as high as 55.29 %. The synthesized supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels exhibited a broad and strong absorption band (500–900 nm). GelMA hydrogels further enhanced the photothermal performance of supra-CNDs. The supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels could rapidly reach a temperature of 50 ℃ within a short period of time (70 s) under irradiation of 808 nm at 1 W/cm2. In addition, the thermal conductivity of the supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels was explored. The thermal properties were found to be more uniform within a diameter of 4 mm from the centre of irradiation. The supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels not only exhibited an excellent photothermal warming effect but also demonstrated exceptional biocompatibility. When melanoma (A-375) cells were cultured with the composite hydrogels, the survival rate exceeded 90 %. However, under 730 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation, the survival rate decreased significantly to 13.6 % and 21.1 %, respectively, thereby verifying the cell-killing ability of the composite hydrogels. The composite hydrogels significantly enhanced the photothermal performance of supra-CNDs material and reduced the laser irradiation time compared to other research results. The outstanding photothermal performance and low toxicity of supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels make them highly potential for photothermal therapy. The optimisation of the mechanical properties of composite hydrogels and the photothermal ablation effect on tumor tissue by supra-CNDs@GelMA composite hydrogels will be further researched in the next step.

Qianqian Duan: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Jing Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Qi Zhang: Methodology, Supervision. Xiaoyuan Wang: Supervision, Methodology. Huaqian Li: Supervision, Methodology. Xing Guo: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Dan Han: Supervision, Methodology. Xiaojuan Wang: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Yanfeng Xi: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Li Guo: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Pengcui Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Juanjuan Xue: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Shengbo Sang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration.
